content:
30 billion devices run it inside. A handful of people keep it safe, and they just made a decision that no security team has ever dared to make in an open source project.

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Raphaël Biscaldi on UnsplashThirty billion installations. More than one security report every day. And a team small enough to fit around one dinner table reads every single one.
That mismatch should have brought the whole thing down years ago. It never did.
Your phone runs curl. So does your car, gaming console, the box streaming your shows, and the servers behind most of the apps you opened today. The small command-line tool that moves data across the Internet turns 30 this year, and for almost all of that time, hardly anyone noticed it was there.
The security reports changed early in 2026. They started arriving faster than any human could read them, good ones and bad ones tangled together because writing a convincing report has become cheap with LLMs. And the man at the center of it warned, in public, that there were no lifeboats coming.
So he did something almost no maintainer has ever done on purpose.
The Lead Took an Unexpected…
